dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, dispatched for breathing difficulty in Richmond. 66-year-old female at an apartment in the city division reports shortness of breath with underlying asthma and hypertension.
Hello, I'm Javier, an EMT with Rescue 3. You initially called 911 for shortness of breath-can you confirm your symptoms today, ma'am?
Yes. I've been having trouble breathing and a cough for days. It's gotten worse overnight, and I needed help finally today.
How aware are you of your surroundings? Please state your name for me.
My name is Lisa, and I am awake with my eyes open.
Good, Lisa. What year is it and how long have you been aware of the symptoms?
It's 2020. I've been aware for about 30 minutes, and I can't move comfortably due to breathing difficulty.
Airway is patent, and you are speaking clearly. Is there a posture that helps you breathe more easily?
Let's quickly evaluate breathing. We get a rate of 34 per minute-your respiration is rapid with significant use of accessory muscles.
Heart rate is 90 and strong. Skin is warm, dry, and well-perfused with good cap refill.
We've noted a pulse of 90 beats per minute and regular rhythm.
When did the shortness of breath and cough begin? And how long have they been getting worse?
Respiration remains at 34 breaths per minute, with clear signs of labored breathing.
Blood oxygen saturation is 97% at this time. That is acceptable and consistent with stable breathing.
Everything began gradually, but over the past week it's gotten progressively worse. The worst was last night; I couldn't rest due to coughing.
Blood pressure is 129 over 77. Levels are normal for now, but we will continue to monitor.
May we ask about medications you are currently taking? We noticed Amlodipine and Albuterol in our report.
I take Amlodipine, Tolterodine, Esomeprazole, Dicyclomine, Sertraline, Amitriptyline, Perphenazine, Montelukast, Atorvastatin, Fluticasone, Albuterol, and Ipratropium.
Thank you for confirming that. It's good documentation for the medical record.
We are giving 0.25 mg of Terbutaline subcutaneously to help your breathing and open the airways.
Blood glucose cannot be read at the moment and shows zero. We will recheck in a moment to confirm.
Skin is warm, dry, and without signs of rashes or discoloration. Looks good aside from your olive pigmentation.
Let me check your eyes. I'll be using a light, okay?
Eyes look normal with equal and reactive pupils. Lisa remains fully aware and answers accurately.
I will check your nose and mouth quickly. Have you had any nose discomfort or mouth sores?
No, just airway symptoms. Nothing in the nose or mouth.
Lung sounds reveal scattered wheezing in the left upper lobe. Chest expansion appears symmetrical overall.
We are attaching leads for EKG. Nothing abnormal seen in the 4-lead tracing at this time.
The 12-lead EKG shows sinus rhythm with no significant ST-segment deviations.
You mentioned a rash on your face. What else did you notice at that time?
The rash started a couple of days ago. I was told to start on antibiotics for it.
What about fever or other signs of an infection while the rash was present?
No fever at all. It was just a rash.
Let's talk about the chest pain-how severe is it on a scale from 0 to 10?
About a 3 it is. It gets worse with coughing. It is sensed in the upper middle part of my chest.
Could you check if you've had any neck pain or stiffness during your episode?
No, my neck remains normal to touch.
Rechecked-still reads zero. No signs of lightheadedness or tremors observed; it's likely an issue with the meter.
Performing ETCO2 capnography to better assess your breathing pattern and CO2 levels.
We are following protocol 3-11 for respiratory distress. We've started Terbutaline which is showing positive effects.
Re-checked pulse: 84 beats per minute and regular.
Respiration rate is now at 30-some improvement since the last check.
Blood pressure has slightly decreased to 113 over 77. Still, nothing alarming to report.
SpO2 has improved to 99 percent. That is definitely a helpful sign of better oxygenation.
Glucose remains at 0 with no measurable changes-confirming earlier suspicion it's an issue with the meter.
Re-examining breathing-less labored now compared to earlier. Terbutaline is working well.
We are transporting you to VCU Health for further testing and care. You will be well taken care of.
We will shift you to the stretcher now, Lisa. Please allow me to assist gently.
Okay, please be careful. I get a bit dizzy sometimes, so I need to be handled softly.
Now moving you to the stretcher with full assistance. Be secure and comfortable.
Results of treatment are clear-improved breathing and reduced use of accessory muscles. EKG remains stable.
Patient is conscious and completely aware at this time, with a GCS of 15 on arrival scene.
Final destination is VCU ED. Medical team is on standby for your arrival.
I will help with the movement from chair to stretcher today. I'll be right beside you for comfort and help.
Please wait-Room 11 will be where you will be moved into soon. Let me call and verify with the hospital.
Rechecking vitals before transport: pulse remains 84, respiration 28, and rhythm still shows no EKG issues.
SpO2 has improved to 100%. Positive trend noted with improved respiration and medication benefits.
ED team, we have a 66-year-old female with a history of asthma and hypertension presenting with respiratory distress.
Vitals are stable after initial treatment. Current readings are pulse 84, respiration 28, BP 107/75, O2 sat of 100.
Patient has a medical history of depression, IBS, and breast cancer. She was not using Albuterol and Atrovent since March.
Note her allergies include Codeine, Guaifenesin, Percocet, and Dilaudid. Advise ED to document those alerts.
Terbutaline was administered at 11:54 and showing positive effects. Breathing improvement observed on transport.
Transport environment has been stable with consistent vitals and no signs of acute distress. Patient orientation is maintained.
Smooth transition to the EMS stretcher was achieved with patient effort and assistance.
She slid onto the stretcher to the cot by herself after being cleared into the room.
We will step through and ensure the ED team knows all critical factors during this handover.
Terbutaline has continued to show positive results upon reassessment. Vitals remain within normal ranges.
ED team is prepared for arrival. Transport environment has been smooth and secure.
ED check-in is in progress now. All documentation and vitals will remain communicated to the team.
Breath work has improved significantly compared to initial assessment. Everything is progressing well.
All transit milestones accomplished with stable vitals and no interruptions in care.
The ambulance is pulling up to VCU now. I will steady the stretcher once the ramp is up.
Expected arrival in under 15 minutes. No delays, and all transport steps were navigated seamlessly.
Room is locked and secured. Care details have been fully transmitted for ED records by protocol.
Final vitals pre-arrival: pulse 84, respiration 28, BP 107 over 75, SpO2 100%. EKG still stable.
Stretcher is moving down the hallway now. Alarms are off. Smooth transport with no changes in condition.
Transport launch ready. Steps to stabilize are minimized due to proactive verbal and patient feedback.
WOB is less apparent, and the patient shows increasing comfort. Breathing improved without intervention.
Any dizziness during this movement, Lisa? We want to make sure for fall risk.
No, I feel more stable now. No more gasping. I knew the wheeze was going to settle down.
All pre-arrival readings are complete. Everything positive and consistent with the last check.
Transport is a success with full compliance. Environment stayed controlled and well-anticipated.
Transition into the ED is now upcoming. Room 11 is confirmed for ED team standby.
Independent movement by patient onto the cot after clearance was successful and without incident.
No distress indicators. Breathing is significantly better and WOB is minimized at this point.
We will stay nearby during handoff. Please remain calm and allow the ED team to take over at the assigned room.
Our team has jointly met and ensured no critical detail was missed. Document transfer is confirmed.
Please inform the ED regarding the rash and prescribed antibiotics. It's likely viral, but not a priority in this episode.
Terbutaline administration was correctly timed and effective. Observations were documented fully in the field.
She slid onto the hospital cot independently after being cleared into the designated room.
All medical history and current status is passed on. Albuterol/Atrovent compliance ending in March was highlighted.
She remains cradled in improvement and stable through the ED room transition.
Sign off and transfer complete. All items discussed with the ED team, including critical vitals and medical alert status.
No further action is necessary during transport. Vitals stabilizing. No lab-exclusive care required.
We are at VCU now. ED personnel are coming for care handover. I'll document and complete the sign-out.
She slid herself directly to the cot portion. No complaints or spontaneous movement distress noted.
Positive peripheral pulses noted during evaluation. Keep monitoring to ensure no changes occur.
Received by ED safely. No additional steps requested. Full stable take-over and care transition completed.
